# PR #244 — Cross-project and cross-agent session portability

This is a large, well-architected feature PR (~8,300 lines of Go across 39 files) adding `specstory resume`, `specstory search`, `specstory reindex`, a `sessions.db` index, real-time live indexing, and per-provider session *reconstruction* (rebuilding native session files from neutral `SessionData` so a session can be resumed in a different agent). Overall code quality is high: dense, well-justified "why" comments, consistent error handling, single-exit-point style, and genuinely strong tests. My findings are mostly minor; nothing blocks merge.

I reviewed everything except the ignored paths (`.specstory/`, `.claude/`, `changelog.md`, etc.) and the `docs/*.md` files.

---

## Line-by-line review

### `main.go`

Lines 9-12, 21-22, 41-43, 75-76, 84-99 (live indexing wired into run/sync/watch)

- (1) ✅ The `NewLiveIndexer(cwd)` + `defer .Close()` + `.Record(...)` pattern is clean and consistently applied across `run`, `sync`, `syncProvider`, and `syncSpecificSessions`. The nil-safe design (methods are no-ops on a nil receiver) keeps the call sites free of guard clauses.
- (2) ✅ Good call making `var liveIndex *cmdpkg.LiveIndexer` (line 33) default-nil for the `--print` path, so `liveIndex.Record(...)` at line 76 is a safe no-op when there's nothing to save. The comment explains why.
- (3) ✅ `foundAgentID` (line 51, 59, 67) is a clear name and correctly threaded so the index records the *provider that actually had* the session.

Lines 104-120 (command registration) — (4) ✅ Straightforward; `resumeCmd`/`reindexCmd`/`searchCmd` wired in alongside the existing commands.

### `pkg/analytics/events.go`

- (5) ✅ Two new events (`EventResumeActivated`, `EventReindexCompleted`) with aligned comments. Consistent with existing conventions.

### `pkg/config/config.go`

Lines 188-242 (`ResumeConfig`, `GetResumeViewMode`, `SaveResumePrefs`)

- (6) ✅ `GetResumeViewMode` defaults to `"dense"` defensively; clear.
- (7) ✅ `SaveResumePrefs` upserts only the `[resume]` block to preserve the self-documenting template — good rationale, documented.

Lines 248-299 (`upsertResumeSection`)

- (8) ⚠️ This is hand-rolled TOML editing (line scanning for `[resume]` and the next `[section]`). It's correct for the current flat template and well-tested, but it's fragile against TOML features like an inline `[resume]` written on a commented line or a `[[resume]]` array-of-tables. Given the input is always your own generated template, the risk is low — worth a one-line comment noting the assumption that section headers are top-level and on their own line. (The `strings.TrimSpace(ln) == "[resume]"` check already enforces this, so it's a documentation nit, not a bug.)
- (9) ✅ Correctly distinguishes "append when absent" vs "replace preserving others," and skips empty keys. Nicely scoped.

### `pkg/spi/global.go`

- (10) ✅ `GlobalSessionRef`, `PathSessionReader`, `ScanReporter`, `ProgressEnumerator` are all clearly documented capability interfaces. The "project is discovered, not supplied" framing is excellent context.
- (11) ✅ `ScanReporter` is nil-safe on every method (`Add`, `Found`) so providers report unconditionally — clean concurrency design using `atomic.Int64`.

### `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go`

Lines 539-577 (`FlattenSessionData`)

- (12) ✅ Single, well-documented home for the cross-provider flattening policy. The rules comment is exactly the kind of "why" documentation that belongs here.
- (13) ✅ Index-based loops (`for i := range data.Exchanges`) taking `&data.Exchanges[i].Messages[j]` avoid copying `Message` structs — idiomatic and efficient.

Lines 585-603 (`syntheticTurnMarkers` / `isSyntheticTurnText`)

- (14) ⚠️ **Inconsistency with the Claude provider's filter.** Here `isSyntheticTurnText` uses `strings.Contains` over its marker set, but `claudecode.isSyntheticMessage` (provider.go:110-122) uses `strings.HasPrefix`. The marker sets also differ: this one has `<TEXTBLOCK>` but **not** `"[Request interrupted by user"`, while the Claude one has the interrupt marker but not `<TEXTBLOCK>` in the prefix loop. Net effects:
  - A genuine user message *containing* (mid-text) `<command-name>` is dropped from a reconstructed transcript (`Contains`) but kept as a title (`HasPrefix`).
  - An `[Request interrupted by user…]` line is excluded from title selection but **can still be replayed** into a reconstructed session, since this filter doesn't list it.
  These are edge cases, not crashes, but the two filters arguably should share one policy. Worth either unifying or adding a comment explaining the deliberate divergence.

Lines 637-658 (`toolTurnText`) — (15) ✅ Falls back to `"Tool use: " + tool.Name` so tool activity is never silently empty. Good defensive default, tested.

### `pkg/utils/project_identity.go`

Lines 846-910 (`EnsureProjectIdentity` rewritten to use `resolveIdentity`)

- (16) ✅ Collapsing `generateWorkspaceID`/`generateGitID`/`generateProjectName` into one walk-up `resolveIdentity` call is a real DRY win and fixes the monorepo-subdirectory fragmentation. `resolvedName` is guaranteed non-empty (falls back to base name), so the old "use workspace_id as name" branch is correctly gone.
- (17) ⚠️ **Behavior change worth calling out:** for *new* projects, `WorkspaceID` is now the hash of the **git root** (canonicalized/lowercased on macOS), not the launch directory. Existing `.project.json` files keep their old value (the `if WorkspaceID == ""` guard prevents rewrite), and `ComputeProjectID` prefers the persisted value — so this is migration-safe. But any external consumer that independently recomputed a launch-dir workspace_id for a *new* monorepo subdir could now disagree. This looks intentional and is well-handled; flagging so it's a conscious decision.

Lines 925-943 (`caseInsensitiveFilesystem` / `canonicalizeWorkspacePath`)

- (18) ✅ Making `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` a `var` purely for test injection is a clean, idiomatic seam, and the comment explaining the Windows inclusion (despite no Windows build) is excellent forward-looking context.

Lines 1035-1092 (`findGitRoot`, `resolveGitConfigPath`)

- (19) ✅ Correct handling of `.git`-as-a-file (worktrees/submodules) including the `commondir` indirection — and it's tested (project_identity_test.go:1236). This is the kind of detail that's easy to get wrong; nicely done.
- (20) ✅ `readOriginURL` consolidates the previously-duplicated regex parsing from `generateGitID` and `generateProjectName`. Good de-duplication; the package-level `originRemoteURLRegex` is compiled once.

Lines 1126-1161 (`ComputeProjectID`, `persistedWorkspaceID`)

- (21) ✅ The precedence — git_id → persisted workspace_id → freshly computed hash — is clearly reasoned in comments and exhaustively tested (8 subtests). This is the crux of cross-project attribution and it's handled carefully.

### `pkg/sessionindex/store.go`

- (22) ✅ Excellent package doc framing `sessions.db` as a derived, deletable cache.
- (23) ✅ `Open` vs `OpenReader` (writerConns=1 vs readerConns=4) with the WAL rationale is a thoughtful concurrency design — single serialized writer, many readers for the interactive UI.
- (24) ✅ `sessionColumns` shared constant keeping SELECT order in lockstep with `scanSession` (line 146-148, 517-532) is a good guard against column-drift bugs.
- (25) ✅ The `IsNew` optimization (lines 57-66, 232-247) skipping the whole-FTS delete for brand-new rows is well-documented and clearly correct (new row ⇒ no FTS row to delete). The "leave false when unsure" guidance is the right default.
- (26) ✅ `prepareUpsert`/`upsertOne`/`UpsertBatch` correctly prepare statements once per transaction and use the committed-flag + deferred-rollback idiom. Clean.
- (27) ✅ `SearchContext` ordering by `updated_at DESC` instead of BM25, with the detailed rationale (length-normalization noise, ISO-8601 = chronological text sort), is a strong, well-justified product decision.
- (28) ✅ `Snippets` deliberately lazy + dedup by `seen` map + STX/ETX markers — good performance-aware design, and the markers avoid colliding with content.
- (29) 💡 Minor: `SessionBody` compares `err == sql.ErrNoRows` directly rather than `errors.Is`. Fine for the modernc driver (it returns the sentinel), but `errors.Is` is the more future-proof idiom. Trivial.

### `pkg/cmd/reindex.go`

- (30) ✅ `reindexVersion` with a changelog-style comment block (lines 41-50) is a great pattern — the fingerprint version is the mechanism that forces re-parse on logic changes, and the history documents *why* each bump happened.
- (31) ✅ `dedupRefs` (lines 221-250): the multi-file-per-session ping-pong explanation is exactly the subtle correctness issue that needed documenting; keeping freshest-by-mtime is the right fix.
- (32) ✅ `processWork` (lines 297-347): worker-pool-parse → single-writer-channel, with `sort.SliceStable` largest-first to overlap the huge-Codex-file stragglers. Good throughput reasoning; the `goto closing` for ctx-cancel is acceptable Go for the fan-in shutdown.
- (33) ✅ `LiveIndexer` (lines 409-502): the `lastSeen` dedup keyed on `UpdatedAt` to skip no-op FTS-heavy upserts is a worthwhile optimization, and the "live row carries no fingerprint so reindex re-verifies it later" comment closes the loop on correctness.
- (34) ✅ `parseFullSession` (557-562) prefers `PathSessionReader` — this is the O(N²)→O(N) fix for Codex, well-explained.
- (35) 💡 `enumerateAll` returns three index-aligned slices (`ids`, `provs`, `perProvider`). It works and is documented, but three parallel slices is slightly error-prone; a `[]struct{id; prov; refs}` would be marginally safer. Not worth changing given it's well-contained.
- (36) ✅ Progress rendering (`reindexProgress`, `scanProgress`) is cleanly separated: workers only bump atomics/observe; a single goroutine draws. TTY vs non-TTY handled. `isTerminal` via `os.ModeCharDevice` avoids an isatty dependency — good.
- (37) ✅ Analytics tracked on completion with useful properties (lines 155-161).

### `pkg/cmd/resume.go`

- (38) ✅ `fprintf`/`fprintln`/`fprint` helpers (37-39) with the "UI writes aren't actionable" justification — clean way to silence errcheck without scattering `_, _ =`.
- (39) ✅ `resumePlan.fromCwd` and its long comment (43-54) capture the cross-project resume fix precisely; `prepareResumeTarget` (274-330) correctly loads the source from `fromCwd` while writing/launching under the current cwd.
- (40) ✅ `writeReconstructedSession` (354-399): temp-file-in-same-dir → fsync → rename → dir-fsync is textbook durable atomic write, and the comment explains the weak-coherence-filesystem motivation. `waitForSessionFileVisible` + `sessionFileReadable` complete the robustness story.
- (41) ✅ `ErrReconstructionUnsupported` handled with a friendly, actionable message (304-308) rather than a raw error.
- (42) 💡 `sessionFileReadable` reads the first line with `bufio.NewReader(f).ReadString('\n')`. If a reconstructed file's first record were enormous and newline-free it would buffer it all — but reconstructed files always have per-record newlines and are small, so this is fine in practice. Noting only for completeness.

### `pkg/cmd/search.go`

- (43) ✅ Cleanly reuses `sessionTUI` with `startInSearch: true` and the shared `launchResume` path — no duplication of the launch logic.
- (44) ⚠️ Line 938: `_ = config.SaveResumePrefs(viewMode, rm.result.targetID)` discards the error here, while the equivalent call in `selectResumeViaTUI` (session_tui.go:1289) logs it at debug. Minor inconsistency; logging in both would be marginally better, but pref-save failure is genuinely non-critical.
- (45) 💡 The agent-enumeration loop (889-900) — building `agents` map + `installed` slice — is duplicated almost verbatim in `selectResumeViaTUI` (1219-1234). A small shared helper (`collectAgents(registry)`) would DRY this up. Low priority.

### `pkg/cmd/session_tui.go`

- (46) ✅ The unified `sessionTUI` model backing both `resume` and `search`, with the differences isolated to `sessionTUIOpts`, is a good design that guarantees feature parity by construction.
- (47) ✅ `ftsQuery`/`fieldTokens`/`segmentTokens` (731-792): the tokenizer-mirroring logic (matching the index's unicode61 splitting so `poem.txt`→`poem + txt*`) is subtle, correct, and has the best test table in the PR. The "only letters/digits survive ⇒ no FTS5 syntax can reach MATCH" note is an important safety property.
- (48) ✅ Debounced async search (`searchDebounceMsg`/`searchResultMsg`/`snippetSeq`) with context cancellation of superseded queries is the right architecture for keeping typing responsive over a slow FTS.
- (49) ⚠️ **Verified-but-fragile:** the pervasive `return m, m.requestVisibleSnippets(modeX)` pattern relies on the mutating pointer-method (`m.snippetSeq++`) running *before* `m` is captured for the return value. I confirmed gc evaluates it in that order (so it works), but the Go spec leaves the ordering of a plain operand vs. a call in the same return statement **unspecified**. If a future toolchain reordered it, snippets/search results would silently stop appearing (seq mismatch ⇒ all results discarded). Calling the mutator on its own line first (`cmd := m.requestVisibleSnippets(...)` then `return m, cmd`) would make it robust and is the more conventional Bubble Tea style. Not urgent, but it's a latent trap given how many call sites depend on it.
- (50) ✅ `relativeTime` (1083-1106) disambiguates prior years (`Jan 2 '06`) and the dense layout shrinks the label by the date overflow (sessionRow lines 916-925) so the turns column can't wrap — nice attention to terminal layout detail.
- (51) ✅ `sessionMarkdown` (1135-1150) degrades gracefully: real specstory render → raw FTS body → "(no readable content)". Good.
- (52) 💡 The agent-filter loop (`if m.agentFilter == "" || s.Agent == m.agentFilter`) appears in `applyFilter` and twice in `applySearchResults` (1626-1649, 1943-1978). A `filterByAgent([]Session) []Session` helper would remove three copies. Low priority.
- (53) ✅ Scope/escape-key semantics (`startedInSearch` vs `startedInBrowser` controlling whether esc quits or backs out) are handled consistently across `updateGlobalSearch`/`updateGlobalResults` with explaining comments.

### Providers — `claudecode`, `codexcli`, `cursorcli`, `geminicli`, `droidcli`, `deepseektui`

- (54) ✅ `path_utils.go`: `encodeProjectDirName` extracted and reused by both `GetClaudeCodeProjectDir` and the new `resolveClaudeProjectDir` (which doesn't require the dir to exist) — good DRY, and the package-level regex is compiled once instead of per-call.
- (55) ✅ `claudecode` `scanClaudeSession` refactor into a struct (`claudeSessionScan`) shared by the project-scoped and global enumeration paths is clean; capturing `cwd` and a `commandFallback` title (so command-only sessions don't show a UUID) is a real UX fix, tested.
- (56) ✅ `codexcli` `scanCodexSessionHeader` (provider.go:855-912): the cheap `userMessageMarker` substring screen before full JSON parse, to skip multi-MB injected-context records, is a smart and well-justified optimization. `GetAgentChatSessionByPath` + `readCodexJSONL(collectRaw bool)` correctly avoid building the giant raw string on the index path.
- (57) ✅ Parallel header scanning in both Claude and Codex enumerators (worker pool over collected paths, mutex-guarded append, `r.Add(1)` counting sessions-not-files) is consistent and correct.
- (58) ✅ The six `ReconstructSession` implementations each emit the minimal native shape needed for that agent's `--resume`, with the dual-stream insight for Codex (response_item *and* event_msg) and the deliberate omission of `environment_context` documented. The hand-rolled protobuf/Merkle-DAG `store.db` builder for Cursor (reconstruct.go:103-276) is impressive and validated against the real `cursor-agent --resume`.
- (59) 💡 The reconstructed-version constants — `reconstructedClaudeModel="claude-opus-4-8"`, `reconstructedClaudeVersion="2.1.178"`, `reconstructedCodexCliVersion="0.139.0"` — are hardcoded "match observed sessions" values that will drift as the agents update. They're documented as best-effort and only affect loader acceptance, but they're a maintenance liability worth a tracking note. (Side note: `claude-opus-4-8` is a current/valid Claude model id, so that one's fine today.)
- (60) ✅ `cursorcli.ListAllAgentChatSessions` honestly leaves `OriginCwd:""` with a clear comment that md5 is one-way and cwd is recovered later via hash reverse-lookup — consistent with the deferred-Cursor-cwd note in your memory. Good that it's documented rather than silently empty.
- (61) ✅ `geminicli.prepareGeminiProjectDir` correctly creates the `.project_root` marker + `chats/` dir for an unseen project, with a basename-collision fallback to the hash dir — and it's the one provider whose `NativeSessionPath` has meaningful side effects, which is documented.

---

## Most important improvements (prioritized)

1. **Unify the two "synthetic message" filters** (obs. 14). `spi.isSyntheticTurnText` (`Contains`, no interrupt marker) and `claudecode.isSyntheticMessage` (`HasPrefix`, with interrupt marker) overlap but diverge in both matching strategy and marker set. The practical gap: `[Request interrupted by user…]` lines can be replayed into a reconstructed session. Either share one policy or add a comment justifying the split.

2. **De-fragilize the TUI return pattern** (obs. 49). `return m, m.requestVisibleSnippets(...)` works under gc but depends on evaluation order the spec doesn't guarantee. Split into `cmd := m.requestVisibleSnippets(...)` / `return m, cmd` at the (many) call sites, or make the seq-bumping explicit before the return. Cheap insurance against a class of "search silently stops working" bugs.

3. **Plan for the hardcoded native-format version strings** (obs. 59). Add a tracking issue/TODO so the Claude/Codex/Cursor version + model placeholders get reviewed when those agents bump their on-disk formats; a stale value could make `--resume` reject reconstructed files.

4. **Small DRY passes** (obs. 45, 52): extract `collectAgents(registry)` (duplicated in `search.go` and `session_tui.go`) and a `filterByAgent` helper (three copies in the TUI). Optional, but they're verbatim repeats.

5. **Document the new-project workspace_id behavior change** (obs. 17) somewhere durable (you have `docs/SESSIONS-DB.md`) so the git-root-based, case-folded workspace_id for new projects isn't a surprise to cloud-sync or other consumers later.

---

## Unit-test assessment

The tests are a genuine strength of this PR — they target real logic and combinatorial behavior rather than coverage theater.

**Strong, non-trivial, well-designed:**

- `TestComputeProjectID` (project_identity_test.go) — the standout. Monorepo collapse, two-clone/url-form equivalence, worktree `.git`-file resolution, remote-less collision, persisted-vs-computed precedence, git_id-wins, empty-cwd. This is an executable spec for the trickiest logic in the PR.
- `TestFTSQuery` / `TestQueryReady` — data-driven, cover the tokenizer-mirroring edge cases (open quotes, filenames, punctuation, non-Latin scripts) that are the whole reason the code is subtle.
- The six provider `TestReconstructSession_RoundTrip` tests — reconstruct → re-parse via the forward path → assert the flattened transcript matches. This is exactly the right way to test a reverse serializer: it pins the round-trip invariant, not the byte format.
- `TestLiveIndexerRecord` — exercises first-record derivation, FTS searchability, the `lastSeen` dedup no-op, in-place update on new activity, and nil-receiver safety. Real behavior, would catch real regressions.
- `TestUpsertIsIdempotent` / `TestSearchAndSnippets` (sessionindex) — verify FTS rows are replaced not duplicated, and snippet markers are emitted. Catches the standalone-FTS-sync class of bug.
- `TestPrepareResumeTargetLoadsSourceFromOriginCwd` — directly guards the named cross-project regression via a `fakeProvider` that records the path it was asked to load from. Precise.

**Reliability:** All pass; no time/network/order flakiness. The case-fold tests correctly save/restore the `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` var. The `fakeProvider` write/visibility tail uses `t.TempDir()` so it's hermetic.

**Gaps worth considering (only if you think the logic warrants it):**

- (a) `ftsQuery`/`isSyntheticTurnText` divergence (obs. 14) isn't pinned by a test. A single case asserting how an interrupt-marker turn flows through *both* filters would lock in whichever behavior you decide is correct.
- (b) `dedupRefs` (the multi-file-per-session ping-pong fix, reindex.go:221) has prose but no direct unit test. It's pure and important enough that a small table — two refs, same `(agent,session_id)`, different mtimes ⇒ freshest wins, no-id dropped — would be worthwhile.
- (c) `upsertResumeSection` is well-tested for append/replace; not tested for an input that *only* contains `[resume]` (no other sections) or a `[resume]` as the final section with trailing blank lines. Minor.

I would **not** add tests for the TUI rendering, the ANSI progress drawing, or the SQLite pragmas — those are either presentation or third-party behavior and tests there would be brittle/tautological.

---

**Bottom line:** Architecturally sound, carefully commented, and well-tested. None of the findings are blockers — items 1 and 2 are the two I'd most want addressed before merge, and both are small.

=== imports ===
// Package config provides configuration management for the SpecStory CLI.
// Configuration is loaded with the following priority (highest to lowest):
//  1. CLI flags
//  2. Local project config: .specstory/cli/config.toml
//  3. User-level config: ~/.specstory/cli/config.toml
//
// Note: For telemetry settings, environment variables (OTEL_*) take highest priority
// per OpenTelemetry conventions.
package config

import (
	"fmt"
	"log/slog"
	"os"
	"path/filepath"
	"strings"

	"github.com/BurntSushi/toml"
)

const (
	// ConfigFileName is the name of the configuration file
	ConfigFileName = "config.toml"
	// SpecStoryDir is the directory name for SpecStory files
	SpecStoryDir = ".specstory"
	// CLIDir is the subdirectory for CLI-specific files (config, auth, etc.)
	CLIDir = "cli"
)

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-06-29 14:39:36Z)**_

Here's what I found, and the fix.

## Obs 8 (TOML): keep the string-based upsert — don't switch to the encoder

I checked. The codebase uses `BurntSushi/toml` **only for reading** (`toml.DecodeFile` at config.go:483, 526). It has **never** written config programmatically — every write emits the static `defaultConfigTemplate` string (config.go:443-444, 472-473). `SaveResumePrefs`/`upsertResumeSection` is the *first* code that writes a value back.

So there's no existing "encode the struct" convention to be consistent with. And switching to `toml.Encode` here would actively regress: BurntSushi's encoder serializes the whole `Config` struct, which would (1) erase every comment in the self-documenting template and (2) turn all the commented-out defaults into active keys (zero-value `*bool`s, empty strings), changing the file's semantics. The string-based upsert exists precisely to avoid that ("preserving the rest of the file… so the self-documenting template stays intact").

Conclusion: the current approach is the *more* consistent one with the codebase's template-based writing. No change — I'll leave obs 8 as a documentation nit only.

## Obs 14 (the two synthetic filters): we need both, but they should share one marker list

**Why both exist** — they run at different layers on different inputs:

- `claudecode.isSyntheticMessage` (provider.go:490) runs on a **single raw Claude record's content** during header scanning, to pick the first *real* user message for the title/slug. The prompt-replacement markers appear at the **start** of the record, so it matches by `HasPrefix` (so a real prompt merely *mentioning* `<command-name>` mid-sentence stays real — there's a test for that).
- `spi.isSyntheticTurnText` (reconstruct.go:115) runs on a **flattened, provider-neutral turn** (content parts already joined) to drop synthetic turns from reconstruction/indexing. A marker can land anywhere in the joined text, so it matches by `Contains`.

So they can't be merged into one function, and the `HasPrefix` vs `Contains` split is correct. But the **marker sets had drifted**, which was the real defect: `spi` was missing `<local-command-stderr>` and `[Request interrupted by user`, so interrupt/stderr lines were stripped from titles but **replayed into reconstructed sessions**.

The fix: make `spi` the single source of truth for the shared command-scaffolding tags, have both functions consume it, and document the deliberate matching difference. Let me apply it.

Clean. Done.

## Summary

**Obs 8 (TOML)** — No change. The codebase uses `BurntSushi/toml` only for *reading*; all writing emits the static self-documenting template. Switching `SaveResumePrefs` to `toml.Encode` would erase the template comments and promote every commented default to an active key. The string-based upsert is the consistent choice — I left it as a documentation nit only.

**Obs 14 (synthetic filters)** — We need both (different layers: raw-Claude-record title selection with `HasPrefix` vs. flattened cross-provider turn filtering with `Contains`), but their marker lists had drifted. Fixed:

- `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go`: introduced `spi.SyntheticCommandTags` as the single source of truth for the six command/local-command tags (now including `<local-command-stderr>`), plus `extraSyntheticTurnMarkers` for `<TEXTBLOCK>` and the interrupt marker. This closes the real gap — `[Request interrupted by user…]` and stderr turns are no longer replayed into reconstructed sessions.
- `pkg/providers/claudecode/provider.go`: `isSyntheticMessage` now consumes `spi.SyntheticCommandTags` (keeping its deliberate `HasPrefix` matching and the warmup/`<TEXTBLOCK>` `Contains` checks), so the two filters can't drift again.
- `pkg/spi/reconstruct_test.go`: extended `TestFlattenSessionData_DropsSyntheticTurns` to lock in that stderr and both interrupt variants are dropped.

All affected tests pass; `golangci-lint` reports 0 issues. (Per your global rules I haven't committed anything — the working tree changes are ready for you.)
